pg10-p1-s4: I had to be, for I was hopeless.
pg10-p1: I cared to have it. I was shocked, but interested. Certainly I was interested. I had to be, for I was hopeless.
pg10-p2-s3:
I could almost hear the sound of the preacher's voice as I sat,
on still Sundays, way over there on the hillside; there was that proffered temperance pledge I never signed; my
grandfather's good natured contempt of some church folk and their doings; his insistence that the spheres really had
their music; but his denial of the preacher's right to tell him how he must listen; his fearlessness as he spoke of
these things just before he died; these recollections welled up from the past.
pg10-p2:
He talked for hours. Childhood memories rose before me. I could almost hear the sound of the preacher's voice as I sat,
on still Sundays, way over there on the hillside; there was that proffered temperance pledge I never signed; my
grandfather's good natured contempt of some church folk and their doings; his insistence that the spheres really had
their music; but his denial of the preacher's right to tell him how he must listen; his fearlessness as he spoke of
these things just before he died; these recollections welled up from the past. They made me swallow hard.
pg10-p3-s1: That war-time day in old Winchester Cathedral came back again.
pg10-p3: That war-time day in old Winchester Cathedral came back again.
pg10-p4-s1: I had always believed in a Power greater than myself.
pg10-p4-s2: I had often pondered these things.
pg10-p4-s4:
Few
people really are, for that means blind faith in the strange proposition that this universe originated in a cipher and
aimlessly rushes nowhere.
pg10-p4-s5:
My intellectual heroes, the chemists, the astronomers, even the evolutionists, suggested vast
laws and forces at work.
pg10-p4-s6: Despite contrary indications, I had little doubt that a mighty purpose and rhythm underlay all.
pg10-p4-s7: How could there be so much of precise and immutable law, and no intelligence?
pg10-p4-s8:
I simply had to believe in a Spirit of the
Universe, who knew neither time nor limitation.
pg10-p4-s9: But that was as far as I had gone.
pg10-p4:
I had always believed in a Power greater than myself. I had often pondered these things. I was not an atheist. Few
people really are, for that means blind faith in the strange proposition that this universe originated in a cipher and
aimlessly rushes nowhere. My intellectual heroes, the chemists, the astronomers, even the evolutionists, suggested vast
laws and forces at work. Despite contrary indications, I had little doubt that a mighty purpose and rhythm underlay all. How could there be so much of precise and immutable law, and no intelligence? I simply had to believe in a Spirit of the
Universe, who knew neither time nor limitation. But that was as far as I had gone.
pg10-p5-s1: With ministers, and the world's religions, I parted right there.
pg10-p5-s2:
When they talked of a God personal to me, who was love,
superhuman strength and direction, I became irritated and my mind snapped shut against such a theory.
pg10-p5:
With ministers, and the world's religions, I parted right there. When they talked of a God personal to me, who was love,
superhuman strength and direction, I became irritated and my mind snapped shut against such a theory.
